notes: Original Year 4 reading assessment starter pack. All passages and questions are original and intended for a static app to iterate through JSON. No copyrighted NFER material is included.

passages.title: The Hidden Garden
passages.text:
When Arun followed the narrow path through Briar Wood, he expected it to end at the river. Instead, it bent sharply behind a clump of hazel trees and led him to a stone arch he had never seen before.

The arch was half covered in ivy. At its centre hung a rusted iron ring. Arun brushed away the leaves and pulled. With a groan, the wooden gate beneath the ivy swung inward.

Beyond it was a walled garden.

Beds of mint and lavender grew in neat squares. A cracked sundial leaned to one side, and a tiny pond reflected a slice of grey sky. Nothing moved except a robin pecking at the path.

Arun took one careful step inside. Whoever had made this place had once cared for it deeply. Even now, although weeds pushed through the gravel, the garden did not feel wild. It felt waiting.

At the far end stood a glasshouse with clouded panes. Arun could just make out the shapes of shelves inside. He glanced back towards the wood. The sensible thing would be to fetch his sister and come back later.

Yet the garden seemed to have kept its secret for years, and for one bright moment he wanted the secret all to himself.

passages.title: How Sundials Work
passages.text:
A sundial tells the time by using the position of the Sun. Long before clocks became common, people used sundials in gardens, courtyards and town squares.

At the centre of a sundial is a raised piece called a gnomon. When sunlight falls on it, the gnomon casts a shadow. As the Sun appears to move across the sky, the shadow shifts. The markings on the dial show the hour.

Sundials work best on bright days. On cloudy days, the shadow may be faint or disappear completely. They also need to be placed carefully so they point in the correct direction. A badly positioned sundial will give the wrong time.

Although sundials are simple, they helped people organise daily life for hundreds of years. Today, they are still used in some gardens and schools, not because they are the most accurate timekeepers, but because they show how people once measured time using nature.

questions.stem: What was hidden beneath the ivy?
acceptedAnswers: A wooden gate
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does the word 'groan' suggest when the gate opened?
acceptedAnswers: It opened noisily
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: Why does Arun take only one careful step into the garden?
acceptedAnswers: He is cautious because the place is unfamiliar and mysterious.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for recognising caution/uncertainty linked to the strange garden. Award 1 mark for a partial idea such as 'he was careful' without explanation.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Arun steps carefully because he does not know what is inside the hidden garden and feels both curious and unsure.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: He was careful because it was a strange place.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: He was tired from walking.

questions.stem: Why does the writer say the garden did not feel wild. It felt waiting?
acceptedAnswers: To make the garden seem as if it is holding a secret or expecting someone.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for explaining that the garden feels alive/expectant/mysterious. Award 1 mark for noting that it seems unusual or special.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The writer makes the garden seem almost alive, as though it has been waiting for someone to discover it, which adds mystery.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: It makes the garden sound special.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It means the plants were growing quickly.

questions.stem: What is the raised piece in the centre of a sundial called?
acceptedAnswers: A gnomon
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: In the sentence 'They also need to be placed carefully', what does 'They' refer to?
acceptedAnswers: Sundials
questions.markingNotes: Award 1 mark for 'sundials' or equivalent.
questions.modelAnswerGold: It refers to sundials.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: The clouds.

questions.stem: Why might a sundial stop being useful on a cloudy day?
acceptedAnswers: Because the shadow becomes faint or disappears, so the time cannot be read.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for linking clouds to loss of shadow and difficulty telling time. Award 1 mark for only one of these ideas.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Without strong sunlight, the gnomon cannot cast a clear shadow, so the hour markings cannot be used properly.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: There is no shadow to read.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Because the metal gets wet.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of the final paragraph?
acceptedAnswers: Sundials are still valued today because they show how people once measured time.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for the full main idea. Award 1 mark for 'people still use sundials today' without the reason.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The final paragraph explains that people still use sundials today because they are a useful link to how time was measured in the past.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: Some sundials are in gardens today.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Sundials are more accurate than clocks.

questions.stem: What is Arun most likely to do next? Use the text to help you.
acceptedAnswers: He is likely to explore a little further, probably towards the glasshouse, because he wants the secret to himself.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for a sensible prediction supported by evidence from the final paragraph. Award 1 mark for an unsupported but plausible prediction.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Arun will probably explore further, perhaps looking inside the glasshouse, because the hidden garden excites him and he wants a moment of discovery for himself.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: He will lock the gate and go home immediately.

questions.stem: Why can a badly positioned sundial give the wrong time?
options: It points in the wrong direction
acceptedAnswers: It points in the wrong direction
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does the writer create a mysterious tone in the fiction passage?
acceptedAnswers: By describing hidden features, quiet movement and the feeling that the garden has been keeping a secret.
questions.markingNotes: Award 3 marks for identifying two or more methods with clear explanation. Award 2 marks for one explained method. Award 1 mark for a simple comment such as 'it sounds mysterious'.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The writer creates mystery by showing the hidden gate under ivy, the silent garden with almost nothing moving, and the idea that the place is 'waiting' and has kept a secret for years.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: The writer uses hidden things and quiet details to make it mysterious.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: The writer makes it funny by using a robin.

questions.stem: How are the two passages linked by the idea of time?
acceptedAnswers: The fiction passage includes an old sundial in a forgotten garden, while the nonfiction passage explains how sundials were used to tell time.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for linking the sundial in the story to the explanation text. Award 1 mark for noticing both mention a sundial or old things.
questions.modelAnswerGold: They are connected because the story includes a cracked sundial in the hidden garden, and the nonfiction passage explains what a sundial does and why people used one.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Both happen in the same place.

passages.title: At the Bird Hide
passages.text:
By the time the school minibus reached the nature reserve, the sky had already turned the colour of wet slate. Leila pressed her forehead to the window and watched reeds shiver beside the lake.

“Ten minutes,” said Mr Kemp, “and then we head for the bird hide.”

The class hurried out with their waterproofs zipped to the chin. At first, the rain was only a mist. Then the wind arrived.

It came skimming across the water, flattening the reeds and pushing ripples towards the shore. A loose information board banged against its post. Somewhere above them, unseen geese called sharply.

Leila and her partner, Ben, ducked into the hide just as the rain thickened. Inside, it smelled of damp wood and mud. Through the narrow window, the lake looked completely different now—darker, rougher, almost angry.

Ben pointed to a cluster of black dots near the far bank. “Coots,” he whispered.

Leila raised her binoculars, but a sudden blast rattled the wooden wall. She jumped, then laughed at herself.

Strangely, the storm made everything easier to notice: the silver slant of rain, the white flash of a bird’s wing, the way the clouds seemed to lean low over the water. The reserve no longer felt like a quiet outing. It felt like a place with its own weather and its own mood.

passages.title: How Storms Form
passages.text:
Storm clouds form when warm, moist air rises and cools. As it cools, the water vapour in the air turns into tiny droplets, which gather to form clouds.

If the air keeps rising, the cloud can grow taller and denser. In some storms, the top of the cloud reaches very high into the atmosphere. These giant clouds can produce heavy rain, thunder, lightning and strong winds.

Wind often becomes stronger before and during a storm because air is moving quickly from one place to another. Differences in temperature and pressure can cause this movement. Over lakes or open fields, strong wind may feel even more powerful because there are fewer buildings or trees to slow it down.

Meteorologists, who study weather, use radar and satellites to track storms. This helps them warn people when severe weather is approaching. Although storms can be dramatic to watch, they can also be dangerous, so it is important to listen to weather warnings and take shelter if needed.

questions.stem: Where did Leila and Ben shelter from the storm?
acceptedAnswers: In the bird hide
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does the word 'rattled' suggest in the sentence 'a sudden blast rattled the wooden wall'?
acceptedAnswers: It shook noisily
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does Leila feel about the storm by the end of the passage?
acceptedAnswers: She feels excited or fascinated rather than simply scared.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for recognising interest/excitement with support from final paragraph. Award 1 mark for a simple feeling word without support.
questions.modelAnswerGold: By the end, Leila seems fascinated by the storm because she notices more and sees the reserve as full of mood and drama.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: She is bored.

questions.stem: Why does the writer describe the lake as 'almost angry'?
acceptedAnswers: To make the stormy lake seem powerful and full of mood.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for explaining personification/effect. Award 1 mark for noticing it sounds rough or wild.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The writer makes the lake seem almost like a living thing so the storm feels stronger and more dramatic.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: It shows the water was rough.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It means someone was in the lake.

questions.stem: What do meteorologists use to track storms?
acceptedAnswers: Radar and satellites
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'severe weather' mean in the final paragraph?
acceptedAnswers: Very strong or dangerous weather
questions.markingNotes: Award 1 mark for 'very strong weather', 'dangerous weather' or equivalent.
questions.modelAnswerGold: It means weather that is very strong and can be dangerous.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Weather that lasts a long time.

questions.stem: Why might wind feel stronger near a lake than in a town?
acceptedAnswers: Because there are fewer buildings or trees near the lake to slow the wind down.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for the full causal explanation. Award 1 mark for saying there is less to block the wind.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Near a lake, the wind can blow across open space without many obstacles, so it feels stronger than it would in a built-up town.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: There are no walls to stop it.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: The water makes the wind colder.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of paragraph 3?
acceptedAnswers: It explains why wind often becomes stronger before and during storms.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for this idea. Award 1 mark for a weaker summary such as 'it is about wind'.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Paragraph 3 explains why strong winds happen in storms and why they feel especially strong in open places.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It says all storms happen over water.

questions.stem: What is most likely to happen after this passage ends?
acceptedAnswers: The class will stay sheltered until the storm eases or an adult tells them it is safe to move.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for a sensible prediction based on the worsening storm and safety. Award 1 mark for a plausible unsupported prediction.
questions.modelAnswerGold: They will probably remain in the bird hide for a while because the storm is getting worse and taking shelter is the sensible choice.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: They might wait in the hide.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: They will go swimming in the lake.

questions.stem: What happens when warm, moist air rises and cools?
acceptedAnswers: It turns into droplets
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does the writer make the storm feel dramatic?
acceptedAnswers: By using strong weather details, sounds and personification to show the reserve changing mood.
questions.markingNotes: Award 3 marks for two or more explained features. Award 2 marks for one clearly explained feature. Award 1 mark for a general comment.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The storm feels dramatic because the writer uses details such as banging boards, rattling walls and clouds that seem to lean low over the lake, making the setting feel alive and intense.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: The writer uses strong words to make it dramatic.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: The writer uses a lot of names.

questions.stem: How does the nonfiction passage help you understand the fiction passage better?
acceptedAnswers: It explains how storms form and why wind feels stronger in open places like the reserve.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for linking storm formation/open spaces to the story. Award 1 mark for saying it gives facts about storms.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The nonfiction text explains the science behind what Leila experiences, especially the strong wind over the open lake and the storm clouds overhead.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It tells us Leila was right to bring binoculars.

passages.title: The Blue Door
passages.text:
The note was folded into a square so small that Tomas nearly missed it. It had been pushed beneath the lid of the old biscuit tin where his grandmother kept buttons, bits of string and odd keys that opened nothing.

He unfolded the paper carefully.

Find the blue door before sunset.

There was no name, no map and no explanation.

Tomas looked around the attic. Dust floated through a beam of light from the roof window. Trunks stood against one wall; picture frames leaned against another. There was certainly no blue door.

He almost laughed. Then he noticed that one of the trunks had left a pale rectangle on the floorboards, as though it had not always stood where it was now. With effort, he dragged it aside.

Beneath it was a narrow trapdoor with a round handle painted blue.

A shiver ran along Tomas’s arms. The note had not been a game after all.

He knelt and lifted the trapdoor. A cool draft rose from below, carrying the smell of stone and rain. Steps disappeared into darkness.

Grandma was downstairs hanging washing in the garden. Tomas could call her. He probably should. Yet the attic, which had seemed ordinary just minutes earlier, now felt full of unfinished stories. Gripping the note in one hand, he placed his foot on the first step.

passages.title: Codes and Clues
passages.text:
Codes and clues have been used for centuries to keep messages private or to guide people to a hidden place. A simple clue might describe an object or place in a puzzling way. The reader must work out what the clue really means.

Treasure hunts often use a chain of clues. One clue leads to the next, and each step reveals a little more information. This makes the challenge more exciting because the person solving it must notice details, make sensible guesses and test ideas.

More secret systems, such as ciphers, change the letters in a message according to a pattern. For example, each letter might be moved forward by one place in the alphabet. A becomes B, B becomes C, and so on. Without knowing the pattern, the message can be difficult to read.

Today, codes are still used in different ways. Computers use complex codes to protect information, while games and escape rooms use clues and puzzles to create mystery and fun.

questions.stem: Where did Tomas find the note?
acceptedAnswers: In an old biscuit tin
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does the word 'ordinary' mean in the final paragraph?
acceptedAnswers: Normal
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: Why does Tomas decide not to call his grandmother straight away?
acceptedAnswers: He is too curious and wants to explore the mystery himself first.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for linking curiosity to the mystery. Award 1 mark for 'he wanted to see' or similar without fuller explanation.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Although he knows he probably should call her, Tomas is pulled on by curiosity because the note and hidden trapdoor make him want to discover the secret himself.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: He forgot where she was.

questions.stem: What effect does the phrase 'unfinished stories' have?
acceptedAnswers: It suggests the attic holds secrets from the past and creates mystery.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for explaining mystery/hidden past. Award 1 mark for saying it makes the attic interesting.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The phrase makes the attic feel full of secrets and old events that have not been explained yet, which deepens the mysterious mood.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: It makes the attic seem exciting.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It means books were left open.

questions.stem: What does a chain of clues do in a treasure hunt?
options: It leads from one clue to the next
acceptedAnswers: It leads from one clue to the next
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'cipher' mean in this passage?
acceptedAnswers: A secret system for changing a message
questions.markingNotes: Award 1 mark for a clear equivalent definition.
questions.modelAnswerGold: A cipher is a secret way of changing a message so it is harder to read.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: A locked chest.

questions.stem: Why do treasure hunts usually use more than one clue?
acceptedAnswers: Because several clues make the challenge more exciting and require more thinking.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for both excitement and problem-solving. Award 1 mark for one of those ideas.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Treasure hunts use several clues so the solver has to keep noticing details and making guesses, which makes the activity more exciting.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Because one clue is too small to read.

questions.stem: How does the passage develop from paragraph 1 to paragraph 4?
acceptedAnswers: It moves from simple clues, to treasure hunts, to ciphers, and then to how codes are used today.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for a clear sequence. Award 1 mark for a partial sequence.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The text starts with simple clues, then explains clue chains in treasure hunts, then introduces ciphers, and finally shows that codes are still used today.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: It starts with treasure and ends with computers.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It goes backwards in time.

questions.stem: What is most likely to happen next in the story?
acceptedAnswers: Tomas will go down the steps to investigate the hidden space.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for a prediction supported by the final sentence. Award 1 mark for a plausible unsupported prediction.
questions.modelAnswerGold: He will probably begin exploring below the trapdoor because he has already lifted it and put his foot on the first step.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: He will go down the steps.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: He will go straight to bed.

questions.stem: In the example cipher, what happens to the letter A?
acceptedAnswers: It becomes B
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does the writer build suspense in the story?
acceptedAnswers: By revealing clues gradually and ending with Tomas about to explore the darkness.
questions.markingNotes: Award 3 marks for two or more methods with explanation. Award 2 marks for one well-explained method. Award 1 mark for a simple statement like 'it is suspenseful'.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Suspense is built by the strange note, the hidden blue handle under the trunk, the cool draft from below and the final image of Tomas stepping into the darkness.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: The writer reveals clues slowly and ends at an exciting moment.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: The writer tells us the ending immediately.

questions.stem: How does the nonfiction passage connect with the fiction passage?
acceptedAnswers: It explains how clues and hidden messages work, which helps the reader understand the note Tomas finds.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for linking clues/codes to Tomas's note and mystery. Award 1 mark for saying both are about clues.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The nonfiction passage explains the idea of clues and puzzles, so it helps us see Tomas's note as the start of a mystery or treasure-hunt-style challenge.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: They are both written by the same person.

passages.title: The Vixen Under the Bench
passages.text:
The fox appeared just as Nia was clipping the dead heads from the marigolds. It slipped between the compost bin and the fence, paused beneath the apple tree and stared straight at her.

For a second, neither of them moved.

Then Nia noticed the fox was limping.

Its left front paw barely touched the ground. The animal looked thinner than she expected too, with a ragged patch of fur near one shoulder. It turned to leave, but not before glancing back towards the shed.

Nia followed its gaze.

Beneath the old wooden bench beside the shed was a dark shape. She crept closer. Two tiny fox cubs were curled together in a nest of dry leaves. One lifted its head and made a weak, squeaking sound.

Nia’s breath caught. The vixen had brought her cubs into the garden.

She backed away slowly and called for Dad. Within minutes, the two of them were peering from the kitchen window. Dad frowned. “We shouldn’t go too near,” he said. “If she feels trapped, she may not come back.”

Together they phoned a local wildlife rescue centre. The volunteer who answered asked careful questions and told them to keep the garden quiet, leave fresh water nearby and watch from a distance.

All afternoon, Nia checked the window. At dusk, the fox returned, moving carefully but carrying food in her mouth. Nia felt relieved. The garden, which had always been tidy and ordinary, had suddenly become part of a wild and secret life.

passages.title: Living with Urban Wildlife
passages.text:
Urban wildlife is the name given to wild animals that live in towns and cities. Although many people think of cities as crowded places made of roads and buildings, they can still provide food, shelter and safe routes for animals.

Foxes are one example of an animal that has adapted well to urban life. Gardens may offer quiet places to rest, while bins and compost heaps can provide food. Hedgehogs, blackbirds, squirrels and some bats also make use of parks, hedges and even small gaps between fences.

People can help urban wildlife by planting flowers, leaving shallow dishes of water, avoiding litter and making spaces easier for animals to move through. For example, small holes in garden fences can allow hedgehogs to travel safely from one garden to another.

Living alongside wild animals can be exciting, but it also means acting responsibly. Animals should not be crowded or handled unless trained experts advise it. Watching quietly from a distance is often the best way to help.

questions.stem: Where were the cubs hiding?
acceptedAnswers: Under a wooden bench
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does the word 'limping' tell you about the fox?
options: It is moving awkwardly because it is hurt
acceptedAnswers: It is moving awkwardly because it is hurt
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: Why does Nia back away slowly after finding the cubs?
acceptedAnswers: She does not want to frighten the mother fox or put the cubs in danger.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for both caution and concern for the animals. Award 1 mark for a partial idea.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Nia backs away because she realises the fox family needs space and she does not want to scare the vixen or make things worse.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: She wanted to hide the cubs.

questions.stem: Why does the writer call the fox family's life 'wild and secret'?
acceptedAnswers: To show that a hidden natural world exists close to Nia's ordinary home.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for hidden/natural world idea. Award 1 mark for 'it sounds mysterious'.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The phrase shows that even in a tidy garden there is a hidden world of animals living their own lives, which feels both natural and mysterious.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: It makes the garden sound interesting.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It means nobody knew where the shed was.

questions.stem: Which animal is mentioned as using small holes in garden fences?
acceptedAnswers: Hedgehogs
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'adapted' mean in paragraph 2?
acceptedAnswers: Changed to live successfully in a new environment
questions.markingNotes: Award 1 mark for an equivalent meaning.
questions.modelAnswerGold: It means changed or adjusted so the animal can live successfully in that place.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Became larger.

questions.stem: Why might gardens be useful places for foxes in towns?
acceptedAnswers: Because they can provide quiet shelter and sources of food.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for shelter and food. Award 1 mark for one of these.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Gardens can help foxes because they offer places to rest and may also provide food from bins, compost heaps or other sources.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: They are always empty.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of paragraph 3?
acceptedAnswers: People can make towns and gardens more helpful for urban wildlife.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for a clear summary. Award 1 mark for 'people can help animals'.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Paragraph 3 explains practical ways people can support urban wildlife in places such as gardens.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Cities are dangerous.

questions.stem: Why is Nia likely to keep watching from the window rather than going into the garden?
acceptedAnswers: Because the rescue centre advised her to keep her distance and she wants the fox to return safely.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for advice + safety. Award 1 mark for one of these ideas.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Nia will probably keep watching from indoors because she has been told not to crowd the fox family and she wants to avoid scaring them away.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: She will stay inside because she is interested.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: She is not allowed outside.

questions.stem: What should people avoid doing unless experts advise it?
acceptedAnswers: Handling animals
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does the writer show a change in the way Nia sees her garden?
acceptedAnswers: The garden changes from an ordinary tidy place into somewhere connected to wildlife and mystery.
questions.markingNotes: Award 3 marks for identifying the contrast and explaining it. Award 2 marks for partial explanation. Award 1 mark for noticing it changes.
questions.modelAnswerGold: At first the garden is just a normal place where Nia works, but by the end it feels important and exciting because it has become part of the fox family's hidden life.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: She thinks it is more interesting by the end.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: She decides gardens are untidy.

questions.stem: How does the nonfiction passage support the events in the fiction passage?
acceptedAnswers: It explains why foxes may use gardens and why people should watch quietly from a distance.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for both links. Award 1 mark for one link.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The information text explains exactly why the fox might choose Nia's garden and why the rescue volunteer tells the family not to go too near.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: It says foxes live in cities.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It explains how to build a shed.

passages.title: The Model Ship
passages.text:
The museum's upstairs gallery was almost empty when Samira slipped inside. She had come to sketch the old ship figurehead for her art homework, but something else caught her eye first.

At the far end of the room stood a model of a polar ship trapped in painted ice. Its tiny ropes and sails were covered in dust. A label beside it read:
Endurance – Antarctic Expedition

Samira leaned closer. The ship looked sturdy, yet the ice around it rose in jagged blue-white slabs, as if the sea had frozen solid in a single fierce moment.

“Imagine being there,” murmured Grandad, who had offered to bring her. “Months of cold. No easy way home.”

Samira pictured the real ship groaning under pressure from the ice. She imagined men stepping onto a frozen world that shone in every direction. It sounded brave, but also lonely.

She opened her sketchbook, yet instead of drawing the carved figurehead she began to copy the tiny ship. The more she looked, the more details she saw: crates on the deck, a little lifeboat, a flag no bigger than her thumb.

When a museum volunteer walked over, Samira asked if the expedition had succeeded.

“In some ways,” the woman replied. “They did not complete their original journey, but the crew survived.”

Samira nodded slowly. The answer stayed with her. Success, she thought, was not always about reaching the first goal. Sometimes it meant enduring long enough to find another way.

passages.title: What Are Polar Expeditions?
passages.text:
Polar expeditions are journeys made into the Earth's coldest regions: the Arctic in the north and Antarctica in the south. Explorers have travelled there to map coastlines, study wildlife, collect scientific information and test how humans survive in extreme conditions.

These journeys are difficult for many reasons. Temperatures can fall far below freezing, strong winds make it feel even colder, and thick ice can block ships from moving. During parts of the year, darkness may last for long periods, making work harder and affecting mood.

Explorers prepare carefully before setting off. They need warm layered clothing, strong tents, reliable food supplies and emergency equipment. Modern teams also use radios, satellite tools and weather forecasts, which make polar travel safer than it was in the past.

Even today, polar expeditions are demanding. However, the information gathered on these journeys helps scientists learn more about climate, oceans and the animals that live in these remote places.

questions.stem: Why did Samira first come to the museum gallery?
acceptedAnswers: To do art homework
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'enduring' mean in the final paragraph?
acceptedAnswers: Lasting through difficulty
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does Samira feel about the polar expedition model?
acceptedAnswers: She feels fascinated and thoughtful, with admiration for the explorers.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for fascination/admiration with support. Award 1 mark for a partial feeling.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Samira is fascinated by the model and seems to admire the explorers, although she also realises how lonely and difficult the journey must have been.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: She is angry with the museum.

questions.stem: Why does the writer describe the ice as 'jagged blue-white slabs'?
acceptedAnswers: To help the reader picture the ice as hard, sharp and dangerous.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for image + danger. Award 1 mark for saying it helps the reader picture the ice.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The description makes the ice seem sharp and powerful, so the expedition feels more dangerous and real.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: It makes the ice sound colourful.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It means the ice was melting.

questions.stem: Which polar region is in the south?
acceptedAnswers: Antarctica
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: In the last paragraph, what does 'these remote places' refer to?
acceptedAnswers: The polar regions, such as the Arctic and Antarctica
questions.markingNotes: Award 1 mark for polar regions/Arctic and Antarctica.

passages.title: Every Bolt and Beam
passages.text:
The bridge over Mill Brook had always been there, or so it seemed. It was a simple footbridge: metal rails, wooden boards, peeling green paint. Most people crossed it without slowing down.

Not Eli.

Ever since the council had put up a notice saying the bridge would close for repairs, he had begun to study it properly. On his walk home, he stopped to look at the bolts holding the rails in place and the thick metal beams underneath. He pressed his shoe against one board and listened to its hollow knock.

On Saturday, he returned with a notebook.

His aunt Priya, who designed buildings, smiled when she saw him crouching by the stream. “Inspecting it?”

“Trying to understand it,” Eli said.

Priya pointed to the triangular metal shapes along the side. “Those help spread the weight,” she explained. “Bridges are all about forces. Push, pull, bend, hold.”

Eli wrote the words down carefully.

When a cyclist crossed, the bridge trembled slightly, but it did not seem weak. It seemed busy. Every piece was doing a job at the same time.

By the time they headed home, Eli had filled two pages. The repairs no longer felt annoying. They felt important. If the bridge was going to carry people safely for years, then every bolt, board and beam mattered.

passages.title: How Bridges Stay Strong
passages.text:
Bridges are built to help people and vehicles cross obstacles such as rivers, valleys or roads. Different kinds of bridges are used for different jobs, depending on the distance to cross, the weight to carry and the materials available.

A beam bridge is one of the simplest types. It uses a horizontal beam supported at each end. Small footbridges are often beam bridges. A truss bridge uses a pattern of triangles to spread forces through the structure. This helps the bridge stay strong while using less material.

Engineers must think carefully about forces when designing a bridge. Some parts are pushed together in compression, while others are pulled apart in tension. A successful design spreads these forces safely so that no single part takes too much strain.

Before a bridge is repaired or replaced, engineers inspect it closely. They check for rust, cracks, worn surfaces and weak joints. This keeps bridges safe and helps them last longer.

questions.stem: Why did Eli start studying the bridge more closely?
options: It was due to close for repairs
He had to cross a river
acceptedAnswers: It was due to close for repairs
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'inspect' most nearly mean in this passage?
acceptedAnswers: To look at carefully
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

acceptedAnswers: Because he wants to learn about the bridge and record what he notices.

questions.modelAnswerSilver: He wants to write things down.

questions.modelAnswerGold: Eli is likely to feel pleased and interested when it reopens because he now sees the bridge as a carefully designed structure, not just something people walk across.

questions.markingNotes: Award 3 marks for explaining the progression clearly. Award 2 marks for partial explanation. Award 1 mark for a general point.
questions.modelAnswerGold: At first Eli notices the closure notice, but then he studies the bridge, listens to how it sounds, writes down ideas and finally realises every part matters, which shows growing respect.

passages.title: The Square in Bloom
passages.text:
Martha had never liked arriving early, but on the morning of the community planting day she was the first child in the square. The benches were stacked with spades, gloves and trays of young plants with labels she could barely pronounce.

An orange-vested volunteer waved. “Would you mind helping sort the seedlings?”

Martha nodded, though she knew almost nothing about gardening.

Soon the square filled with people. A baker from the corner shop brought a crate of empty boxes for carrying compost. Two older boys fetched water from the tap by the church gate. A woman with a baby strapped to her chest knelt to loosen the soil around the flowerbeds.

At first, Martha kept to the edge, placing labels in neat rows. Then she noticed that one tray had been set in the wrong place. It contained shade-loving plants, yet the bed beside the fountain was in full sun. She remembered hearing the volunteer explain that different plants needed different conditions.

“Should these go near the wall instead?” she asked.

The volunteer looked surprised, then pleased. “Exactly right.”

After that, people kept asking Martha where things should go. By lunchtime, her knees were muddy and her ponytail had come loose, but the square looked transformed. The bare beds had become strips of green and silver. Bees were already nosing around the lavender.

As she packed away the labels, Martha realised she was no longer the child who had arrived too early. She felt like someone who belonged to the place she had helped change.

passages.title: Why Pollinators Matter
passages.text:
Pollinators are animals that carry pollen from one flower to another. This helps many plants produce seeds, fruit and new growth. Bees are among the best-known pollinators, but butterflies, moths, hoverflies and even some birds can do this job too.

Different pollinators are attracted to different kinds of flowers. Bright colours, strong scents and nectar are all useful in drawing them in. A garden with a variety of plants is more likely to attract a range of pollinators across the year.

People can support pollinators by planting flowers that bloom at different times, avoiding harmful chemicals and leaving some areas slightly wild. A shallow dish of water and a mix of sunny and sheltered spots can also help.

Pollinators are important not only for gardens but for farms and orchards as well. Without them, many crops would be harder to grow. Protecting pollinators helps both wild plants and the food people eat.

questions.stem: What job is Martha first asked to do?
acceptedAnswers: Sort the seedlings
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'transformed' mean in the passage?
acceptedAnswers: Changed greatly
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does Martha's confidence change during the passage?

questions.modelAnswerGold: At first Martha is unsure and keeps out of the way, but when she correctly notices where the plants should go she becomes more confident and involved.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: She becomes lonely.

questions.stem: Why does the writer mention Martha's muddy knees and loose ponytail?

questions.modelAnswerGold: It means a mixture or range of different plants.

questions.modelAnswerGold: A mixed garden offers different colours, scents and nectar sources, so it can attract a wider range of pollinators over time.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Because bees only like big gardens.

passages.title: The Delayed Train
passages.text:
The train was due at 9:14, but by 9:20 the platform display still flashed DELAYED in stubborn yellow letters. Jonah shifted his rucksack from one shoulder to the other and tried not to look annoyed.

Mum checked the timetable again. “There’s been a signalling problem.”

Across the tracks, a freight train rolled slowly past, every wagon covered in brown dust. Jonah watched it disappear beneath the footbridge. He had planned the whole day carefully: train to the city, river ferry to the science centre, then lunch before the robotics show. Delays did not fit the plan.

At last, their train arrived with a sigh of brakes. When they boarded, Jonah expected to stay cross, but the carriage windows framed everything like moving pictures. Warehouses slid into allotments; allotments gave way to terraces; terraces opened into a glitter of canal water.

Near the city, the train crossed a high iron bridge. Jonah looked down at roads, rooftops and tiny buses threading through the streets below. For a moment, the journey itself felt like part of the trip rather than a pause before it.

Mum smiled. “Worth waiting for?”

Jonah tried not to grin back. “Maybe a little.”

As the train curved into the station, he realised that transport was not just about arriving somewhere. It also stitched places together, carrying people from one kind of day into another.

passages.title: How Railways Work Safely
passages.text:
Railways are carefully organised systems that move people and goods from place to place. For trains to run safely, many parts of the system must work together, including tracks, signals, stations and timetables.

Signals are one important part of railway safety. They tell drivers when it is safe to move, slow down or stop. Modern railways may use coloured lights and computer systems, but the basic idea is the same: trains must be kept a safe distance apart.

Railways are used for more than passenger journeys. Freight trains carry materials such as stone, timber, fuel and containers of goods. Moving these items by rail can reduce the number of lorries needed on roads.

Although delays can be frustrating, they are sometimes caused by checks or repairs that help keep passengers safe. Weather, busy tracks and technical faults can also affect services.

questions.stem: What was the problem causing Jonah's delay?
acceptedAnswers: A signalling problem
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'stitched places together' suggest in the final paragraph?
acceptedAnswers: Transport links places closely
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.modelAnswerGold: Jonah starts the passage frustrated because his careful plan is disrupted, but he becomes absorbed by what he sees from the train and begins to enjoy the journey itself.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: He becomes more angry.

questions.modelAnswerGold: The comparison turns the view into something vivid and entertaining, showing that Jonah's mood is changing and he is noticing the journey with fresh interest.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: It means the windows were very clean.

questions.commonWrongAnswer: A kind of station.

questions.markingNotes: Award 3 marks for clear explanation of the change. Award 2 marks for partial explanation. Award 1 mark for noting the tone changes.

passages.title: The Gust
passages.text:
On the second morning of the sailing course, the lake looked harmless. The surface was smooth and pale, and only the smallest ripples touched the jetty.

“That usually means the wind will change later,” said instructor Mae, checking the flags onshore.

Rafi was assigned to crew in the smallest training dinghy. He liked the boat's quickness, but not the way it tipped when he moved too suddenly. As they pushed off, he kept his weight perfectly still and watched the sail fill.

For the first few minutes, everything went well. The dinghy cut neatly across the lake. Then a gust swept down from the hill behind the trees.

The boom swung faster than Rafi expected. Water slapped the side of the boat. He grabbed the rope too late and the dinghy heeled sharply, sending cold spray across his sleeve.

“Ease the sheet!” called Mae from the safety boat.

Rafi obeyed, heart thumping. The sail loosened. The dinghy steadied.

By the time they reached the marker buoy, his breathing had slowed. He tried the movement again, this time earlier and more smoothly. The boat answered at once, no longer jerking but turning cleanly through the gust.

Back on shore, Rafi replayed the moment in his head. The gust had not vanished; he had simply learned to feel it sooner. Sailing, he realised, was less about forcing the boat to obey and more about noticing what the wind was already doing.

passages.title: How a Dinghy Sails
passages.text:
Sailing boats move by using the wind. When wind pushes against a sail, it creates a force that helps drive the boat forward. The shape of the sail and the angle of the boat both affect how well this works.

A small sailing boat, such as a dinghy, can react quickly to changes in wind. This can make dinghies exciting to sail, but it also means the sailor must respond quickly. If a sudden gust fills the sail too strongly, the boat may heel over, which means it leans sharply to one side.

Sailors control the sail using ropes called sheets. By pulling in or easing the sheet, they change the angle of the sail to suit the wind. Good sailing is not only about strength. It also depends on balance, timing and careful observation.

Learning to sail often involves practising the same movements many times. As sailors gain experience, they become better at spotting changes in wind and reacting before the boat becomes difficult to control.

questions.stem: Who told Rafi to ease the sheet?
acceptedAnswers: Mae
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'heeled sharply' mean in the story?
options: The boat leaned strongly to one side
acceptedAnswers: The boat leaned strongly to one side
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.modelAnswerGold: At first the gust makes Rafi panic a little and his heart pounds, but after listening and trying again he becomes calmer and responds more smoothly.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: He enjoys falling in.

questions.modelAnswerGold: Dinghies are quick to react, so if a gust arrives the sailor must adjust in time or the boat may heel and become harder to control.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Because the lake is deep.

questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for full idea. Award 1 mark for 'practice helps'.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The final paragraph explains that sailors get better by practising and learning to spot wind changes sooner.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Experienced sailors never make mistakes.

questions.stem: What is Rafi likely to do next time the wind changes suddenly?
acceptedAnswers: He will notice it earlier and adjust the sheet more smoothly.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for prediction + reasoning. Award 1 mark for a plausible prediction only.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Next time, Rafi will probably react sooner because he has realised sailing is about noticing what the wind is doing before the boat becomes hard to control.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: He will jump into the safety boat.

questions.stem: What are the ropes used to control the sail called?
acceptedAnswers: Sheets
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does the writer show learning happening during the sailing lesson?
acceptedAnswers: By showing Rafi repeat the movement more successfully after understanding the gust.
questions.markingNotes: Award 3 marks for explaining the sequence and change. Award 2 marks for a partial explanation. Award 1 mark for a general idea.

questions.commonWrongAnswer: It explains how to build a boat.

passages.title: When the Lights Went Out
passages.text:
When the first stars appeared above the campsite, Poppy expected the usual things: torch beams, crackling wrappers, someone asking where the marshmallows were. She did not expect quiet.

“Power cut,” said Uncle Dan, holding up the dead lantern. “Battery pack too.”

For a moment the whole field seemed to pause. Then a child from the next tent pointed upward.

The sky was crowded.

Without the electric glow from the nearby visitor centre, hundreds more stars had appeared. A pale stripe stretched from one horizon to the other like brushed smoke.

“What’s that?” Poppy whispered.

“The Milky Way,” said Uncle Dan.

People who had been fussing over stoves and folding chairs began to stand still. Even the younger children stopped chasing one another and stared. Poppy lay back on the picnic blanket. Above her, the stars did not twinkle politely the way they did from her street at home. They blazed.

Soon, Uncle Dan used a paper map of the night sky to point out a bright planet and a crooked line of stars that made part of a constellation. Poppy tried to memorise everything, but the names mattered less than the feeling. The sky seemed enormous, yet strangely close, as if it had been waiting all evening for the campsite lights to disappear.

When the lantern finally flickered back to life, nobody seemed in a hurry to turn it on. The darkness no longer felt empty. It felt full.

passages.title: Seeing More Stars
passages.text:
On clear nights, people can often see stars, planets and patterns called constellations in the sky. However, the number of stars visible depends greatly on how dark the sky is.

Light pollution is caused by artificial light from streets, buildings and vehicles. In bright towns and cities, this extra light makes the sky look paler and hides many stars from view. In darker places, far more stars can be seen.

The Milky Way is the galaxy that contains our Solar System. From Earth, it can sometimes be seen as a faint band stretching across the sky. It is not always easy to spot, especially in places affected by light pollution.

People who enjoy observing the night sky are called astronomers if they study it scientifically, but many others simply enjoy stargazing as a hobby. A star chart or sky map can help people identify constellations and planets more easily.

questions.stem: Why did the campsite suddenly become darker?
options: There was a power cut and dead battery pack
acceptedAnswers: There was a power cut and dead battery pack
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'blazed' suggest about the stars?
acceptedAnswers: They looked bright and powerful
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does Poppy feel about the dark sky by the end of the passage?
acceptedAnswers: She feels amazed and appreciative rather than worried.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for a clear feeling with support. Award 1 mark for a feeling word alone.
questions.modelAnswerGold: By the end, Poppy feels full of wonder because the darkness reveals a sky that seems enormous, alive and beautiful rather than frightening.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: She is annoyed that the lights failed.

questions.stem: Why does the writer say the darkness felt 'full'?
acceptedAnswers: To show that once the lights go out, the night reveals detail, beauty and interest instead of emptiness.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for the full effect. Award 1 mark for 'there was lots to see'.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The writer shows that darkness is not simply empty blackness; it allows the stars, planets and the feeling of space to fill the scene with interest.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: It means the tents were crowded.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It means clouds covered the campsite.

questions.stem: What is light pollution caused by?
options: Artificial light from streets, buildings and vehicles
acceptedAnswers: Artificial light from streets, buildings and vehicles
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: What does 'faint band' mean in paragraph 3?
acceptedAnswers: A pale strip or line
questions.markingNotes: Award 1 mark for an equivalent answer.
questions.modelAnswerGold: It means a pale strip of light across the sky.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: A loud sound in the distance.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: A circle around one star.

questions.stem: Why can people see more stars in darker places?
acceptedAnswers: Because there is less artificial light to wash out the sky.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for linking darkness to reduced light pollution. Award 1 mark for saying the sky is darker.
questions.modelAnswerGold: In darker places, there is less artificial light making the sky pale, so more stars remain visible.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: Because there are more stars there.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Because the air is colder.

questions.stem: What is the main idea of paragraph 2?
acceptedAnswers: It explains what light pollution is and how it affects what we can see in the sky.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for the full idea. Award 1 mark for one part only.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Paragraph 2 explains that artificial light creates light pollution, which hides many stars from view.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It says all cities are bad.

questions.stem: What is Poppy likely to do on another camping trip after this experience?
acceptedAnswers: She may try to look at the stars again and perhaps use a sky map.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for a sensible prediction linked to the passage. Award 1 mark for a plausible prediction only.
questions.modelAnswerGold: Poppy will probably want to stargaze again because she was deeply impressed by the dark sky and watched Uncle Dan use a sky map to identify what they could see.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: She will look at the stars again.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: She will stay inside the tent all evening.

questions.stem: What can help people identify constellations and planets?
options: A star chart or sky map
acceptedAnswers: A star chart or sky map
questions.markingNotes: Award full marks for the correct option only.

questions.stem: How does the writer turn an unexpected problem into a positive experience?
acceptedAnswers: By showing that the power cut removes artificial light and reveals a more beautiful sky.
questions.markingNotes: Award 3 marks for clear explanation of contrast and result. Award 2 marks for a partial explanation. Award 1 mark for a general point.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The writer begins with a minor problem — the lights failing — but then shows that this creates the conditions for everyone to notice the stars, making the event feel magical rather than inconvenient.
questions.modelAnswerSilver: The power cut becomes a good thing.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: Nothing changes after the power cut.

questions.stem: How does the nonfiction passage explain why the sky looked different in the fiction passage?
acceptedAnswers: It explains light pollution and why more stars can be seen when artificial lights are off.
questions.markingNotes: Award 2 marks for linking reduced light pollution to the fiction. Award 1 mark for saying both mention stars.
questions.modelAnswerGold: The information text explains that once the campsite and visitor-centre lights went out, there was less light pollution, so many more stars and even the Milky Way became visible.
questions.commonWrongAnswer: It says planets are brighter than stars.
